title: Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption: Across countries in 2024, the share of workers in informal employment plotted against GDP per capita reveals a strong negative relationship, where higher income levels coincide with lower rates of informal work. The horizontal axis measures GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale ranging from $2,000 to $100,000, while the vertical axis tracks the percentage of employed people in jobs lacking basic social or legal protection, spanning from 0% to 100%. Circle sizes correspond to population, making India the largest visible marker. The data points cluster into two distinct groups: a dense collection of African and South Asian nations in the upper-left quadrant and a spread of European and North American nations in the lower-right quadrant. At the highest end of informality, the Democratic Republic of Congo, Chad, Tanzania, and Ethiopia all sit above the 80% mark with GDP per capita below $3,000. Other countries in this high-informality, low-income band include Somalia, Pakistan, and Ghana. In the middle of the distribution, countries like Indonesia, Egypt, and Thailand show informality rates between 60% and 70% with GDP per capita ranging from $10,000 to $20,000. Further down the curve, Mexico, Argentina, and Brazil occupy the 30% to 50% range with incomes between $20,000 and $30,000. At the lowest end of the scale, Germany, Spain, and North Macedonia show informality rates below 10% with GDP per capita exceeding $40,000. South Korea and Russia also appear in this low-informality band, with shares around 20% to 30% and incomes near $50,000. The visible pattern shows a consistent downward slope where increases in GDP per capita align with decreases in the share of informal employment.
